abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as the prevailing approach for text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) generation, yet production platforms must increasingly serve both modalities on shared GPU clusters while meeting stringent latency SLOs. Co-serving such heterogeneous workloads is challenging: T2I and T2V requests exhibit vastly different compute demands, parallelism characteristics, and latency requirements, leading to significant SLO violations in existing serving systems. We present GENSERVE, a co-serving system that leverages the inherent predictability of the diffusion process to optimize serving efficiency. A central insight is that diffusion inference proceeds in discrete, predictable steps and is naturally preemptible at step boundaries, opening a new design space for heterogeneity-aware resource management. GENSERVE introduces step-level resource adaptation through three coordinated mechanisms: intelligent video preemption, elastic sequence parallelism with dynamic batching, and an SLO-aware scheduler that jointly optimizes resource allocation across all concurrent requests. Experimental results show that GENSERVE improves the SLO attainment rate by up to 44% over the strongest baseline across diverse configurations.

citations.fullCitation: Jiarui Fang, Jinzhe Pan, Xibo Sun, Aoyu Li, and Jiannan Wang. xdit: an inference engine for diffusion transformers (dits) with massive parallelism. arXiv preprint arXiv:2411.01738, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper is a key baseline as GENSERVE is implemented using its sequence parallelism library (xFuser). GENSERVE directly addresses the limitations of xDiT's static parallelism and fixed scheduling strategies, which are shown to be inefficient for dynamic, mixed workloads of images and videos.
citations.title: xdit: an inference engine for diffusion transformers (dits) with massive parallelism

citations.fullCitation: Runyu Lu, Shiqi He, Wenxuan Tan, Shenggui Li, Ruofan Wu, Jeff J Ma, Ang Chen, and Mosharaf Chowdhury. Tetriserve: Efficiently serving mixed dit workloads. 2026.
citations.justification: TetriServe is a direct conceptual predecessor that introduced step-level resource management and dynamic sequence parallelism for homogeneous diffusion model workloads. GENSERVE extends this core idea by developing coordinated preemption and scheduling mechanisms specifically for the challenges of co-serving heterogeneous text-to-image and text-to-video requests.
citations.title: Tetriserve: Efficiently serving mixed dit workloads

citations.fullCitation: Yifei Xia, Fangcheng Fu, Hao Yuan, Hanke Zhang, Xupeng Miao, Yijun Liu, Suhan Ling, Jie Jiang, and Bin Cui. Tridentserve: A stage-level serving system for diffusion pipelines. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.02838, 2025.
citations.justification: This work represents an alternative design for diffusion model serving that decomposes the pipeline into coarse-grained stages for resource allocation. It is a relevant citation because it contrasts with GENSERVE's finer-grained, step-level approach, which enables more agile scheduling decisions like preemption and dynamic batching within the main denoising loop.
citations.title: Tridentserve: A stage-level serving system for diffusion pipelines

citations.fullCitation: Peiqi Yin, Jiangyun Zhu, Han Gao, Chenguang Zheng, Yongxiang Huang, Taichang Zhou, Ruirui Yang, Weizhi Liu, Weiqing Chen, Canlin Guo, et al. vllm-omni: Fully disaggregated serving for any-to-any multimodal models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.02204, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as important related work in hybrid serving systems, focusing on disaggregating multimodal LLM pipelines. It is relevant because it tackles the general problem of serving heterogeneous models, but its approach is tailored to LLMs, highlighting the novelty of GENSERVE's techniques (e.g., step-level preemption) which are specifically designed by exploiting the unique predictable and iterative structure of diffusion models.
citations.title: vllm-omni: Fully disaggregated serving for any-to-any multimodal models

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of Heterogeneous Diffusion Model Workloads

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of Heterogeneous Diffusion Model Workloads" was authored by:

*   **Fanjiang Ye, Zhangke Li, Xinrui Zhong, Ethan Ma, Russell Chen, Kaijian Wang, Jingwei Zuo, Yuke Wang**: Rice University
*   **Desen Sun**: University of Waterloo
*   **Ye Cao**: Independent Researcher
*   **Triston Cao**: NVIDIA
*   **Myungjin Lee**: Cisco Research
*   **Arvind Krishnamurthy**: University of Washington

### 2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape

Diffusion models have emerged as a dominant paradigm for generative tasks, particularly text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) generation, with architectures such as U-Net and Diffusion Transformers (DiT). As these models gain widespread adoption, their deployment in production environments necessitates efficient serving systems capable of handling high demand under various Service Level Objectives (SLOs).

The existing research landscape for serving large models, including Large Language Models (LLMs) and diffusion models, has focused on optimizing aspects such as parallelism, scheduling, and caching. Techniques like sequence parallelism, patch-level pipelining, and dynamic resource allocation have been explored to enhance throughput and reduce latency for specific model types or homogenous workloads. For diffusion models specifically, prior work has investigated stage-level GPU allocation, step-level dynamic Sequence Parallelism (SP) adjustment, and caching of intermediate states to reduce computational cost.

However, a notable gap exists in the co-serving of *heterogeneous* diffusion model workloads. Production platforms are increasingly required to serve both T2I and T2V requests on shared GPU clusters. These two modalities exhibit significant differences in computational demands, parallelism characteristics, and latency requirements. Current serving systems, primarily designed for homogeneous workloads, often employ static configurations for batching and parallelism, and utilize conventional scheduling policies (e.g., First-In-First-Out (FIFO) or Shortest-Job-First (SJF)). These static and generic approaches lead to inefficiencies, such as head-of-line (HOL) blocking for latency-sensitive image requests when co-located with long-running video tasks, or starvation of video tasks under SJF.

GENSERVE addresses this specific challenge by developing a co-serving system that accounts for the distinct characteristics of T2I and T2V workloads. It leverages the predictable nature of diffusion model inference and its inherent preemptibility at step boundaries to enable dynamic, heterogeneity-aware resource management. This work positions itself as a system designed to optimize SLO attainment in a mixed-modality environment, a scenario where existing solutions demonstrate limitations.

### 3. Key objectives and motivation

The primary objective of this work is to develop an efficient co-serving system, named GENSERVE, capable of handling heterogeneous text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) diffusion model workloads on shared GPU clusters while meeting stringent latency Service Level Objectives (SLOs).

The motivation for this research stems from several challenges observed in current production platforms:

1.  **Heterogeneous Workload Demands**: T2I and T2V requests exhibit significant differences in compute requirements and latency targets. T2V requests, especially for higher resolutions, involve orders of magnitude greater per-step runtime costs (up to 20x for 720p resolution) compared to T2I requests. These distinct demands are poorly managed by existing serving systems that typically apply a single, fixed serving configuration.
2.  **Ineffective Scheduling Policies**: Conventional scheduling policies fail to balance the competing latency targets of heterogeneous workloads.
    *   **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)**: Long-running video requests can cause severe head-of-line (HOL) blocking, delaying latency-sensitive image requests and leading to SLO violations for images.
    *   **SJF (Shortest-Job-First)**: Prioritizes shorter image requests, potentially starving video requests and causing them to miss deadlines.
    Neither policy effectively minimizes overall SLO violations in a mixed-modality environment.
3.  **Limitations of Static Resource Management**: Existing diffusion serving systems often employ static strategies for batching and parallelism, configured for a single task type. This approach is suboptimal when the workload mix, request resolutions, and device states fluctuate over time. An optimal configuration for one modality may be inefficient for another.
4.  **Underutilization with Separate Deployments**: While T2I and T2V models can share a common Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone and are compact enough to fit on a single GPU without extensive model parallelism, naively dedicating separate GPU pools for each modality leads to underutilization of resources. Co-serving on a unified cluster is attractive for higher utilization but requires sophisticated resource coordination.

To address these issues, the authors identified key opportunities:

1.  **Predictable Diffusion Execution**: Unlike Large Language Models (LLMs) whose inference runtime can be unpredictable due to variable output lengths, diffusion models perform a fixed number of denoising steps with highly stable per-step costs. This predictability allows for accurate estimation of remaining execution time for in-flight requests, enabling resource-aware decisions at a fine-grained, step-level granularity.
2.  **Natural Step-Level Preemptibility**: Diffusion models are inherently iterative, executing denoising steps sequentially. Inference can be paused at step boundaries and resumed later without loss of progress or quality, as the intermediate state (a compact latent tensor) can be retained in GPU memory. This capability facilitates low-overhead preemption, allowing urgent, latency-sensitive tasks to temporarily utilize resources monopolized by longer-running tasks.
3.  **Exploiting Distinct Computational Profiles**: T2I and T2V workloads have different scaling behaviors with respect to batching and parallelism. T2I tasks, especially at lower resolutions, can benefit significantly from dynamic batching to improve GPU utilization and throughput. T2V tasks, particularly at higher resolutions, can leverage elastic Sequence Parallelism (SP) to reduce per-step latency, where the optimal SP degree depends on resolution and computational load. Recognizing these distinct profiles allows for adaptive, workload-aware resource reallocation.

By leveraging these insights, GENSERVE aims to transform the reactive problem of handling heterogeneous workloads into a proactive optimization challenge, where scheduling decisions (including batching, parallelism, and preemption) are made with a global view to maximize SLO attainment across all concurrent requests.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

GENSERVE is designed as a heterogeneity-aware co-serving system that exploits the predictability and step-level preemptibility of diffusion models to optimize serving efficiency for mixed T2I and T2V workloads. Its architecture consists of five core components: the Scheduler, Profiler, Solver, Allocator, and Controller, which interact in a closed-loop system with Worker GPUs.

**System Architecture Overview (Figure 7):**

*   **Scheduler**: The central orchestrator that records request details (task type, resolution, deadline), maintains per-GPU state, and processes worker events. It initiates scheduling rounds, especially for video requests or when contention arises.
*   **Profiler**: Stores a repository of pre-measured, offline latency estimates for diffusion model operations across various resolutions, batch sizes, and Sequence Parallelism (SP) degrees. This data provides the Solver with accurate per-step cost predictions.
*   **Solver**: The core decision-making component. It receives a global snapshot of the cluster state and pending requests from the Scheduler, queries the Profiler for cost estimates, and performs a joint optimization to determine the optimal resource allocation.
*   **Allocator**: Translates the Solver’s high-level scheduling decisions (e.g., preemption, SP reconfiguration, batching) into concrete GPU assignments and adjustments to the parallelism topology.
*   **Controller**: Dispatches inference tasks and control messages (e.g., pause, resume, reconfigure SP) to the Workers based on the Allocator’s instructions.
*   **Workers**: Execute denoising steps on assigned GPUs and report step-level progress back to the Scheduler.

**Three Coordinated Mechanisms:**

GENSERVE integrates three primary mechanisms that collectively address the challenges of heterogeneous diffusion serving:

1.  **Intelligent Video Preemption:**
    *   **Purpose**: To prevent long-running video tasks from monopolizing GPUs and blocking latency-sensitive image requests.
    *   **Mechanism**: Videos are paused at denoising step boundaries, leveraging the fact that intermediate latent states are compact and can be stored in GPU memory with negligible overhead.
    *   **Candidate Selection**: When image requests are pending and no GPUs are available, the scheduler identifies video tasks with the most "slack" (difference between deadline and estimated completion time, including remaining steps and per-step latency, Equation 3). Videos with positive slack are candidates for preemption, with higher-slack videos being prioritized as "safest victims." Videos with non-positive slack are generally excluded to avoid causing deadline misses.
    *   **"Soft" Preemption**: Instead of full preemption, the system can also downgrade a video's SP degree to free up a subset of GPUs, offering a less disruptive alternative.
    *   **Deadline-aware Resume Policy**: Preempted videos are resumed under two conditions: (1) **Budget-tight**: when the remaining time until the video's deadline falls below its estimated completion time, requiring immediate resumption. (2) **Idle**: when no new urgent image requests are arriving, allowing efficient utilization of resources. Upon resumption, the system can also upgrade the video’s SP degree if additional GPUs are available to accelerate completion.

2.  **Elastic Resource Allocation:**
    *   **Purpose**: To dynamically adapt GPU allocation strategies based on the distinct computational profiles of T2I and T2V workloads, rather than applying a uniform policy.
    *   **Elastic Sequence Parallelism (for Video Tasks)**:
        *   The video generation pipeline is decoupled: the memory-bound VAE decoding stage always runs on a single GPU, while the compute-bound DiT denoising stage (which accounts for over 90% of runtime) is parallelized using Sequence Parallelism (SP).
        *   GENSERVE supports runtime SP degree switching. This allows the system to scale up a video's SP degree when GPUs are abundant (to accelerate completion) or scale down its SP degree when GPUs are scarce (to release resources for other tasks), adapting to changing cluster conditions and optimal SP degrees for different video resolutions.
    *   **SLO-aware Dynamic Batching (for Image Tasks)**:
        *   Image requests of the same resolution can be batched to amortize overheads and improve throughput.
        *   Batch formation is deadline-aware: additional requests are only added to a batch if all requests within that batch (including the new candidate) are projected to still meet their respective deadlines, considering the increased latency of larger batches.
        *   A dynamic wait budget, based on the earliest deadline in the current batch, prevents excessive waiting under heavy load, ensuring a balance between batching efficiency and latency requirements.

3.  **SLO-aware Online Scheduler (Knapsack-style DP algorithm - Algorithm 1):**
    *   **Purpose**: To jointly optimize resource allocation across all in-flight requests (preemption, batching, SP degree selection) in real-time, maximizing overall deadline satisfaction.
    *   **Problem Formulation**: The scheduler aims to maximize the sum of deadline-met flags (Equation 4) for all requests, subject to GPU capacity constraints (Equation 5) for the next scheduling round.
    *   **Adaptive Online Selection**:
        *   **Stage 1: Candidate Construction and Scoring**: For each available GPU budget, an optimal image batch (using an Earliest-Deadline-First heuristic) is constructed and scored based on recoverable count and slack-based utility (Equation 6). For each active video, a set of candidates (e.g., hold, continue at current SP, scale SP up/down, resume) is generated, each scored by its projected completion laxity (Equation 7) and a 'recoverable' flag.
        *   **Stage 2: Knapsack DP over Video Groups**: A dynamic programming approach is used to select the best combination of video candidates. The DP iteratively considers each video group and each candidate, tracking a lexicographic objective: first, maximizing the total number of recoverable requests (those projected to meet their deadline), and then maximizing a cumulative utility score as a tie-breaker (Equation 8). GPU overlap checks ensure valid allocations.
        *   **Stage 3: Select Best Plan with Image Evaluation**: After the video DP, the solver evaluates the terminal states. For the remaining free GPUs, it integrates the best image batching plan, selecting the overall plan that maximizes the combined recoverable count across both modalities. The optimal selections are then materialized into concrete GPU assignments and runtime actions.
    *   **Efficiency**: The DP algorithm runs in O(G ⋅ N ⋅ ¯K) time (where G is the number of video groups, N is GPUs, and ¯K is average candidates per group), designed to be lightweight enough for millisecond-scale online scheduling decisions.

These mechanisms are tightly coupled; for instance, GPUs released by SP downgrade become available for batched image requests, and completed image batches free GPUs that can be used to accelerate video tasks via SP upgrade.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The evaluation of GENSERVE focused on its end-to-end serving performance, an ablation study of its components, and sensitivity analyses. Experiments were conducted on a server with 8x NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs, using Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium for T2I and Wan2.2-T2V-5B for T2V. The primary metric was SLO Attainment Rate (SAR), alongside per-request latency CDFs.

**5.1 End-to-end Performance**

*   **SLO Scale (Figure 10)**: GENSERVE achieved the highest overall SAR across varying SLO strictness (σ from 0.8 to 1.3). At the default σ=1.0, GENSERVE attained 78% SAR, exceeding SRTF (71%) and FCFS (59%). The performance gap widened at moderate slack (e.g., σ=1.1, GENSERVE at 87%, 10 percentage points above SRTF), demonstrating the DP solver's effectiveness in exploiting available slack. GENSERVE consistently achieved high image SAR (100% at σ ≥ 1.0) and maintained a higher video SAR (56% at σ=1.0) compared to SRTF (44%), which suffered from aggressive preemption without coordinated deadline awareness.
*   **Workload Mix (Figure 11)**: GENSERVE's advantage was more pronounced in video-heavy or balanced mixes. In a balanced image-to-video mix, GENSERVE achieved 80% overall SAR, 7 pp above SRTF (73%). Under a video-dominated (heavy) mix, GENSERVE attained 41% overall SAR, significantly outperforming SJF (33%) and SRTF (26%). Its video SAR (31%) was 2.3 times that of SRTF (14%), indicating the DP solver's ability to manage preemption more strategically under heavy video loads.
*   **Arrival Rate (Figure 12)**: GENSERVE maintained the highest SAR across all tested arrival rates (12 to 36 req/min). Its advantage was most apparent at moderate loads (e.g., 87% at 18 req/min, 13 pp above SRTF). While the gap narrowed at the highest load, GENSERVE still led (64% vs. 56% for SRTF), showing robustness under increasing contention.
*   **End-to-end Latency (Figure 13)**: For image requests, GENSERVE significantly reduced tail latency, achieving a p90 turnaround time of 5.8 seconds, a 3.1x reduction compared to FCFS (18.0 seconds). This demonstrates the effectiveness of preemption in eliminating head-of-line blocking. For video requests, GENSERVE reduced median turnaround by 41% (52 seconds vs. 89 seconds for FCFS) through dynamic SP, which accelerates per-step computation. The observed wider video tail for GENSERVE was attributed to multiple preemption cycles for the longest-running videos, a trade-off made by the DP solver to prioritize overall SLO attainment.

**5.2 Ablation Study (Figure 14)**

An incremental ablation study highlighted the individual contributions of GENSERVE's components under skewed resolution distribution:

*   **Baseline (FCFS)**: Achieved 20% overall SAR.
*   **+ Preemption**: Improved overall SAR to 39%, with image SAR rising to 68%. However, video SAR dropped to 10% due to uncoordinated preemption.
*   **+ DP Solver**: Added 19 pp to overall SAR, reaching 58% (94% image, 22% video). The solver’s ability to evaluate net SLO impact before preemption recovered video SAR from 10% to 22%.
*   **+ SP Switching (GENSERVE)**: Provided a final 5 pp gain, bringing GENSERVE to 63% overall (94% image, 32% video). Dynamic SP switching reduced average per-step video latency and provided the DP solver with an additional degree of freedom, allowing acceleration of videos instead of always preempting them, further improving video SAR.

**5.3 Sensitivity Analysis**

*   **Resolution Distribution (Table 5)**: Under a Dirichlet-skewed resolution distribution (high concentration of high-resolution requests), GENSERVE's overall SAR dropped from 79% (uniform) to 62%, but its lead over SRTF (43%) widened from 8 pp to 19 pp. This indicates that GENSERVE's dynamic SP allocation effectively adapts to higher runtime variance by assigning larger SP degrees to high-resolution videos.
*   **DP Scheduler Overhead (Table 6)**: The mean decision time for the DP solver ranged from 0.30 ms (1–2 concurrent request groups) to 0.46 ms (9–12 groups), with a maximum observed time of 1.9 ms. This overhead was consistently less than 0.25% of a single 720p video step (781 ms), confirming its efficiency for online scheduling.
*   **Preemption Overhead (Table 7)**: Pause latency was consistently negligible (≤ 4.2 µs). Resume overhead increased with SP degree (from 0.036 ms at SP=1 to 0.868 ms at SP=8 for 720p), but remained a tiny fraction (< 0.2%) of the baseline step time, validating the low cost of preemption.
*   **Paused Video Memory Overhead (Table 8)**: The memory footprint for a paused VideoState (latent tensor, mask, embeddings) was small, with the largest (720p, 81 frames) consuming only 27.2 MB. This is negligible compared to modern GPU VRAM (96 GB), posing no practical OOM concerns.
*   **SP Reconfiguration Overhead**: Reconfiguration overhead was minimal because NCCL process groups are pre-created, and no cross-GPU memory transfer is needed for paused video states. SP changes accounted for less than 0.4% of total denoising steps.
*   **Model Deployment (Figure 15)**: GENSERVE's replicated co-serving approach consistently outperformed static GPU partitioning (e.g., dedicated 2:6, 3:5, 4:4 image:video GPU splits) across all workload mixes. This demonstrated that dynamically multiplexing all GPUs across both modalities avoids resource fragmentation inherent in static partitioning.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The GENSERVE system represents a notable advancement in the field of serving large generative models, particularly for diffusion models. Its significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

1.  **Enabling Efficient Heterogeneous Workload Co-Serving**: GENSERVE addresses a critical and increasingly common challenge in production environments: simultaneously serving diverse text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) workloads on shared GPU infrastructure. By effectively managing the distinct computational profiles and latency requirements of these modalities, it facilitates resource sharing without compromising Service Level Objectives (SLOs).
2.  **Exploitation of Model-Specific Properties**: The work highlights the strategic advantage of leveraging intrinsic properties of diffusion models—their predictable, step-level execution and natural preemptibility at step boundaries. This insight transforms resource management from a reactive problem into a proactive optimization, enabling fine-grained, intelligent control over GPU allocation.
3.  **Substantial Performance Improvements**: Through its coordinated mechanisms (intelligent video preemption, elastic resource allocation, and an SLO-aware dynamic programming scheduler), GENSERVE demonstrates significant improvements in SLO attainment rate (up to 44% over baselines) and reductions in end-to-end latency for image requests (e.g., 3.1x reduction in p90 latency compared to FCFS). These quantitative gains indicate a more responsive and reliable user experience for generative AI services.
4.  **Optimized Resource Utilization**: By dynamically adapting batching for T2I and Sequence Parallelism (SP) for T2V, and by avoiding static partitioning, GENSERVE maximizes GPU utilization and prevents resource fragmentation. This leads to more efficient use of expensive GPU clusters, which can translate to reduced operational costs for service providers.
5.  **Robustness to Dynamic Workloads**: The online, SLO-aware scheduler is designed to react to fluctuating workload mixes, arrival rates, and resolution distributions. Its ability to make real-time, globally optimized decisions ensures consistent performance even under varying demand patterns, which is crucial for real-world deployments.
6.  **Low Overhead and Practicality**: The evaluation confirms that the overheads associated with the core mechanisms—DP solver decision time, preemption (pause/resume), and SP reconfiguration—are minimal, typically less than 0.25% of a single denoising step. The memory footprint for paused video states is also negligible. This demonstrates the practical viability of GENSERVE's fine-grained resource management approach.

The findings from GENSERVE contribute to the broader understanding of how to design high-performance serving systems for complex, heterogeneous AI workloads. The principles and mechanisms introduced could potentially inform the development of future serving systems for other iterative generative models or multi-modal AI applications where predictable, step-wise execution and diverse resource demands are present.
overview:
The rapid evolution of generative AI has shifted focus from text-to-image (T2I) generation toward more computationally demanding tasks like text-to-video (T2V). While these models often share a common architecture—specifically the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone—the resource requirements for serving them are vastly different. T2V requests often require an order of magnitude more computation and memory bandwidth than T2I requests. For example, generating a single $720p$ video can take up to $20\times$ longer than generating a high-resolution image.

When production clusters attempt to serve these heterogeneous workloads together, conventional scheduling policies often fall short. Simple First-In-First-Out (FIFO) scheduling leads to head-of-line (HOL) blocking, where a long-running video request traps numerous latency-sensitive image requests in the queue, causing them to miss their Service Level Objectives (SLOs). Conversely, prioritizing short jobs (SJF) can lead to the starvation of video tasks. GENSERVE is a co-serving system designed to navigate these trade-offs by exploiting the unique execution characteristics of diffusion models.

![Comparison of scheduling policies: FIFO vs. HeteroFusion (GENSERVE)](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04335v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Comparison between standard FIFO scheduling and GENSERVE (HeteroFusion). While FIFO suffers from head-of-line blocking, GENSERVE utilizes video preemption, batching, and dynamic parallelism to ensure all requests meet their deadlines.*

## The Challenge of Heterogeneous Diffusion Workloads

The transition from traditional U-Net architectures to Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) has unified the underlying mathematical framework for images and videos. As shown in Figure 2, both modalities follow an iterative denoising process where a latent tensor is refined over a fixed number of steps. Despite this architectural similarity, the serving requirements are highly asymmetric.

![Diffusion Transformer (DiT) inference pipeline](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04335v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The generic inference pipeline for DiT-based models. The iterative denoising loop (T to 0) allows for natural break points at each step.*

The fundamental problem in co-serving arises from three factors:
1.  **Latency Sensitivity**: Image requests typically have strict, sub-second or low-second latency targets to maintain user interactivity. Video requests, while allowing for longer turnaround times, still have deadlines that are difficult to hit if resources are fragmented.
2.  **Computational Intensity**: A $720p$ video denoising step is significantly more expensive than a $256p$ or $480p$ image step.
3.  **Resource Staticity**: Existing systems often fix the degree of parallelism (e.g., how many GPUs work on one video) and the batch size at startup. If the workload shifts from mostly images to mostly videos, these static configurations become highly inefficient.

## Leveraging Predictability and Preemptibility

Unlike Large Language Models (LLMs), where the runtime is unpredictable because the model might generate five words or five hundred, diffusion models are remarkably predictable. They perform a pre-defined number of denoising steps (e.g., 20 or 50 steps), and each step has a nearly identical execution time for a given resolution. This predictability allows a system to calculate the exact remaining time for any in-flight request.

GENSERVE also exploits the "step-level preemptibility" of these models. Because the inference is a loop of sequential steps, the system can pause a video task at the boundary of any step. The only state that needs to be preserved is the latent tensor. Unlike the KV cache in LLMs, which can grow to gigabytes, the latent tensor for even high-definition videos is relatively small (often tens of megabytes). This makes preemption—pausing a long video to let a quick image "sneak through"—extremely low-overhead.

## System Architecture

The GENSERVE architecture consists of a centralized control plane and a distributed data plane of GPU workers. The workflow is orchestrated by several key components:

*   **Profiler**: An offline component that measures the latency of different model operations across various resolutions, batch sizes, and degrees of Sequence Parallelism (SP). This data allows the system to predict the cost of future actions accurately.
*   **Scheduler & Solver**: The "brain" of the system. It maintains a global view of all pending requests and active GPU states. It uses a specialized algorithm to decide which tasks to run, which to batch, and which to preempt.
*   **Allocator & Controller**: These components translate the high-level scheduling decisions into low-level GPU commands, such as adjusting the NCCL communication groups for parallel tasks or dispatching new batches.

![GENSERVE System Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04335v1/x7.png)
*Figure 3: Overview of the GENSERVE system architecture, highlighting the interaction between the central scheduler, the profiler, and the GPU workers.*

## Intelligent Video Preemption and Elastic Allocation

One of GENSERVE’s core innovations is its approach to "Slack." Slack is defined as the difference between the request's deadline and its projected completion time:

$$ \text{Slack} = \text{Deadline} - (\text{CurrentTime} + \text{RemainingSteps} \times \text{Latency}_{\text{step}}) $$

When image requests start piling up, the system identifies video tasks with the most positive slack—these are the "safest victims." By preempting these videos, the system frees up GPUs to clear the image queue without causing the video tasks to miss their own deadlines. Once the image "burst" is handled, the videos are resumed.

### Elastic Sequence Parallelism (SP)
Sequence Parallelism splits the spatial or temporal dimensions of a tensor across multiple GPUs. GENSERVE implements *Elastic SP*, which allows a video's parallelism degree to change during execution. If the cluster is idle, a video might scale up to use 8 GPUs (SP=8) to finish faster. If a flurry of image requests arrives, the system can "downgrade" that video to SP=2, releasing 6 GPUs for image processing without stopping the video entirely.

### SLO-aware Batching
For image tasks, throughput is often improved by batching multiple requests together. However, batching increases the latency of each individual request in the batch. GENSERVE’s scheduler only forms a batch if every request in that batch is still projected to meet its deadline despite the increased processing time.

## The SLO-aware Joint Scheduler

The scheduler solves a optimization problem: how to allocate $N$ GPUs among $G$ video groups and a set of image requests to maximize the number of satisfied SLOs.

$$ \text{Maximize} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{R}} \mathbb{1}(\text{Completion}_i \leq \text{Deadline}_i) $$

To solve this in real-time (under 2ms), GENSERVE uses a Knapsack-style dynamic programming (DP) algorithm. It treats each video as a "group" and each possible allocation (preempt, continue at SP=2, scale to SP=4, etc.) as an "item" within that group. The DP algorithm finds the combination of choices across all videos that leaves the optimal amount of GPU resources for image batches, ensuring the maximum total number of requests (both image and video) hit their deadlines. The complexity of this solver is $O(G \cdot N \cdot \bar{K})$, where $\bar{K}$ is the average number of allocation candidates per video.

## Performance and Evaluation

GENSERVE was evaluated against several baselines, including FCFS and Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF), using high-end NVIDIA GPUs and state-of-the-art models like Stable Diffusion 3.5 and Wan2.2.

### SLO Attainment
In scenarios with mixed image and video traffic, GENSERVE achieved an overall SLO Attainment Rate (SAR) of $78\%$, significantly outperforming SRTF ($71\%$) and FCFS ($59\%$). Notably, GENSERVE was able to maintain a $100\%$ success rate for image requests while still meeting the deadlines for $56\%$ of video requests—a task where SRTF failed by being too aggressive with preemptions.

![SLO Attainment across different scales](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04335v1/x10.png)
*Figure 4: SLO satisfaction rates across varying SLO strictness (represented by $\sigma$). GENSERVE consistently maintains a higher SAR than conventional scheduling policies.*

### Tail Latency Reduction
For image requests, GENSERVE reduced the $p90$ tail latency by $3.1\times$ compared to FCFS. By eliminating the head-of-line blocking caused by videos, the system ensures that image users receive their results with consistently low delay. For videos, the use of dynamic SP allowed the system to reduce the median turnaround time by $41\%$, as shown by the comparison between $52$ seconds (GENSERVE) and $89$ seconds (FCFS).

### Ablation and Robustness
The ablation study (Figure 5) demonstrates the necessity of each component. Adding simple preemption to a baseline FCFS system improves image performance but hurts video. The addition of the DP Solver recovers the video performance by making preemption "smarter," and Elastic SP provides the final boost by allowing for more granular resource adjustments.

![Ablation study of GENSERVE components](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04335v1/x14.png)
*Figure 5: Incremental performance gains from adding preemption, the DP scheduler, and elastic SP switching.*

The system also showed remarkable efficiency. The memory overhead for storing paused video states was found to be less than $27.2\text{ MB}$ per video, which is negligible on a $96\text{ GB}$ GPU. Furthermore, the time taken by the scheduler to make a decision was typically less than $0.5$ ms, ensuring that the system spends its time generating content rather than debating how to schedule it.

## Conclusion

GENSERVE addresses the growing complexity of serving modern generative models by treating T2I and T2V as a unified, heterogeneous resource management problem. By leveraging the inherent predictability and preemptibility of diffusion models, it moves away from static, modality-specific deployments toward a fluid, SLO-aware co-serving model. The result is a system that can handle the high-throughput demands of image generation while still reliably delivering high-quality video content on shared infrastructure. This approach not only improves user experience by hitting more deadlines but also maximizes the economic efficiency of expensive GPU clusters.

keyInsights: Diffusion model inference is highly predictable due to a fixed number of denoising steps with stable per-step costs, enabling accurate runtime estimation for proactive scheduling.

Diffusion models are naturally preemptible at step boundaries because their compact intermediate latent states can be stored in GPU memory with negligible overhead, allowing for fine-grained resource redistribution.
T2I and T2V workloads have distinct computational profiles and parallelism characteristics (e.g., T2I benefits from batching, T2V from SP) that can be exploited for adaptive resource management.
originalProblem: Existing serving systems struggle to manage the disparate computational demands and latency targets of T2I and T2V diffusion model requests on shared infrastructure.
Conventional scheduling policies (e.g., FIFO, SJF) cause head-of-line blocking for latency-sensitive image requests or starvation for long-running video tasks, leading to SLO violations.
Static resource management and dedicated GPU partitioning for different modalities result in suboptimal GPU utilization and resource fragmentation.
results: GENSERVE achieved up to 78% overall SLO Attainment Rate (SAR) at a default strictness (σ=1.0), outperforming SRTF (71%) and FCFS (59%), with the highest SAR across varying workloads and arrival rates.
It reduced the p90 tail latency for image requests by 3.1x compared to FCFS (from 18.0s to 5.8s), effectively mitigating head-of-line blocking.

The overheads for the DP solver, preemption (pause/resume), and SP reconfiguration were consistently low, typically less than 0.25% of a single 720p video denoising step, confirming practical efficiency.
solution: GENSERVE employs an SLO-aware online scheduler using a knapsack-style dynamic programming algorithm to jointly optimize resource allocation for all in-flight requests.
It integrates intelligent video preemption at denoising step boundaries to free up GPUs for urgent image tasks while considering video deadlines and minimal overhead.
The system utilizes elastic resource allocation, dynamically adjusting Sequence Parallelism (SP) for video tasks and applying SLO-aware dynamic batching for image tasks.
summary.summary: GENSERVE is a co-serving system for heterogeneous text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) diffusion model workloads on shared GPU clusters. It improves Service Level Objective (SLO) attainment rates by up to 44% and reduces image request tail latency by 3.1x through dynamic resource allocation that leverages model-specific execution properties.

title: GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of Heterogeneous Diffusion Model Workloads
